title: The Shape of Writing
subtitle: Point, line, plane, and why signed writing does not need to fit into a line
summary: Presentation 2 in The Science of Sutton SignWriting series.
synopsis: Writing is not inherently linear. Some systems organize language in bounded units, some through sequence, and some through spatial composition. SignWriting belongs in that wider study of how writing uses space, and Formal SignWriting shows how plane-based writing can be stored and processed as text.
cover_image_alt: Cover image for The Shape of Writing in The Science of Sutton SignWriting series.

publication_plan.doi_strategy: publish as distinct citable artifacts connected by series identity and related identifiers
publication_plan.zenodo_record_title_suggestion: The Shape of Writing: Point, Line, Plane, and Why Signed Writing Does Not Need to Fit into a Line
publication_plan.citation_stub: Slevinski, S. (2026). The Shape of Writing: Point, Line, Plane, and Why Signed Writing Does Not Need to Fit into a Line. The Science of Sutton SignWriting.

related_identifiers.note: Formal SignWriting provides the computational and standards context for the spatial encoding discussed here.

slides.title: Writing Is Not Inherently Linear
slides.summary: Writing systems differ in how they organize language in space, and that difference matters.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for the shape of writing.

image_prompts.concrete: Academic keynote graphic showing three writing geometries: a compact character block, a horizontal line of text, and a spatial sign composition, clean white background, elegant and modern
image_prompts.abstract: Point, line, and plane emerging from one luminous field of symbols, geometric and scholarly, no text

bullets: Writing does not have one default shape
Some systems integrate structure into units
Some systems unfold through sequence
Some systems compose meaning in space
slides.speech:
Most people assume writing is basically a line. Marks proceed one after another, meaning unfolds in order, and the page simply holds the sequence. That assumption makes sense because so much modern literacy works that way. But it is only one shape of writing, not the definition of writing.

Writing systems differ in how they organize language in space. Some concentrate structure into integrated units. Some depend mainly on ordered sequence. Some compose meaning spatially across a plane. Once we see that, SignWriting stops looking like a strange exception and starts looking like part of a broader science of writing. That is the move I want to make today.
slides.notes:
Use this slide to break the default assumption gently.

Key lines:
- writing is familiar as a line, but not defined by the line
- the real question is how language is organized in space
- that shift turns SignWriting from anomaly into case study

If speaking off the cuff:
- say that most literate adults have been trained so thoroughly on line writing that they mistake familiarity for universality
- then name the three shapes simply: unit, sequence, space

Transition:
- now define the analytic lens before anyone starts treating it as a rigid taxonomy.

slides.title: A Simple Lens
slides.summary: Point, line, and plane are simple analytic tendencies for understanding how writing uses space.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for point, line, and plane model.

image_prompts.concrete: Minimal academic diagram comparing point line and plane ways of arranging writing with one Chinese character, one ordinary alphabetic sentence, and one spatial SignWriting signbox layout, clean labels and modern design
image_prompts.abstract: Geometric progression from dot to line to field, suggesting increasingly spatial organization of meaning

bullets: Point: integrated graphic unit
Line: sequence-first writing
Plane: spatially composed writing
These are tendencies, not rigid script boxes
slides.speech:
Language Without Sound argued that language does not require sound and that literacy does not require a single sensory channel. This talk asks the next question: what shape can writing take? My proposal is a simple lens: point, line, and plane.

I do not mean this as a rigid taxonomy that traps every script in one permanent box. I mean it as a way to describe the main way a writing system uses space. Is the written form mainly an integrated unit, mainly a sequence, or mainly a spatial composition? That lens is simple enough to use and flexible enough to stay honest.
slides.notes:
Bridge clearly to Language Without Sound.

Key points:
- first talk removed the sound requirement
- this talk removes the linearity requirement
- point, line, and plane are analytic tendencies, not rigid boxes

If you need a plain-language fallback:
- say the framework asks what readers and writers primarily rely on: whole unit, sequence, or spatial arrangement

Transition:
- begin with point because it is the easiest place to show that integration and decomposition can coexist.
slides.mermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[Writing Geometry]
  A --> B[Point]
  A --> C[Line]
  A --> D[Plane]

  B --> E[Chinese character at the unit level]
  C --> F[Alphabetic or similar text line]
  D --> G[SignWriting signbox]

  E --> H[Dominant spatial tendency]
  F --> H
  G --> H

  classDef core fill:#f5f3ff,color:black,stroke:#7c3aed,stroke-width:3px;
  classDef node fill:#eff6ff,color:black,stroke:#2563eb,stroke-width:2px;
  class A,H core;
  class B,C,D,E,F,G node;

slides.title: Point: Integrated Units
slides.summary: Point-like writing compresses structure into a bounded unit, even when that unit can still be analyzed or indexed.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for point-like writing systems.

image_prompts.concrete: Scholarly diagram of a single Chinese character treated as one bounded unit, with internal components lightly indicated and side notes about radicals or stroke indexing, emphasis on integrated whole, white background
image_prompts.abstract: Multiple shapes collapsing into one compact symbol, suggesting compression and internal structure

bullets: A bounded unit carries integrated structure
Internal parts may still matter
Chinese characters are useful at the unit level
Decomposition for indexing does not make the writing linear
slides.speech:
Point-like writing compresses structure into a bounded graphic unit. Internal parts may matter historically, graphically, semantically, or morphologically, but the unit is still approached as an integrated whole rather than as a simple chain.

Chinese characters are useful here at the unit level. I am not claiming that Chinese writing is non-sequential at the sentence level. The narrower point is that a character can function as an integrated written unit and still be decomposed for technical handling. Radicals, components, and stroke counts help with sorting, indexing, and lookup. That does not make the writing linear. It shows that integration and decomposition can coexist, which becomes important again later when we talk about plane writing and computational encoding.
slides.notes:
Keep the example narrow and precise.

Say explicitly:
- point does not mean primitive or simple
- it means the written unit is approached as a bounded whole
- technical decomposition does not erase that whole-unit status

Useful fallback example:
- a library can index a character by radical or stroke count without changing what the reader experiences on the page

Transition:
- now move to the much more familiar line model so the contrast feels intuitive.

slides.title: Line: Sequence and Rendering
slides.summary: Line-based writing depends on order, while most layout is delegated to typography and shaping.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for line-based writing systems.

image_prompts.concrete: Clean academic diagram of a writer entering an alphabetic sequence that becomes rendered line text through a font and shaping engine, modern and legible
image_prompts.abstract: A sequence of marks unfolding across a luminous line and becoming ordered text

bullets: Order and adjacency are central
Writers author sequence
Fonts and shaping engines handle most layout
This is the familiar modern model
slides.speech:
Line-based writing is the familiar modern case. Meaning unfolds through order. Characters or words appear in sequence, adjacency matters, and interpretation depends heavily on progression and parsing.

One reason line writing feels so natural is that we rarely notice the split between what the writer provides and what typography finishes. The writer provides a sequence. Typography, shaping, and layout systems handle much of the visible result. Ligatures, combining marks, and joining behavior may create local visual complexity, but the dominant model is still sequence first. That matters because it shows why SignWriting is not simply waiting for a better font. The issue is not prettier rendering. The issue is a different way of writing.
slides.notes:
This is the stabilizing slide.

Key points:
- order and adjacency do most of the work
- the writer authors sequence, not full spatial relation
- typography handles much of the visual outcome

If you need an off-the-cuff explanation:
- say that in ordinary print the writer gives the system a line, then the font and layout engine make it look right
- that is very different from composing the layout directly

Transition:
- move from familiar line writing to the genuinely different logic of the plane.

slides.title: Plane: Spatial Composition
slides.summary: Plane-based writing makes position, relation, and simultaneity part of the writing itself.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for plane-based writing systems.

image_prompts.concrete: Diagram of a simple SignWriting sign with handshape, movement arrows, and facial markers arranged inside a visible signbox, caption that spatial relations are part of the writing, clean scholarly design
image_prompts.abstract: Symbols floating across a plane with meaningful spatial relation and tension

bullets: Position matters
Relation matters
Grouping and simultaneity can matter
The writer composes the layout directly
slides.speech:
Plane-based writing uses space directly. Position is meaningful. Relative arrangement is meaningful. Grouping is meaningful. In some systems, simultaneity is meaningful. The writer is not only selecting symbols. The writer is composing their relations.

That is why SignWriting matters here. A written sign is not simply a row of labels for handshape, movement, contact, facial expression, orientation, and location. It is a spatial composition in which those relations are part of the written form. This is the key shift in perspective: SignWriting is not an odd line script with unusual symbols. It is a genuine case of plane-based writing.
slides.notes:
Slow down a little here.

Key points:
- define plane by meaningful relation, not by visual complexity alone
- emphasize authorship: the writer composes the layout
- bring SignWriting in as a clear example, not as a special pleading case

Useful fallback wording:
- in line writing, space mostly holds the sequence
- in plane writing, space helps make the writing mean what it means

Transition:
- before introducing technical terms, separate visible writing from software representation.

slides.title: Two Functional Phases
slides.summary: Plane writing for humans and serialized encoding for software are related, but they are not the same layer.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for human writing and machine encoding.

image_prompts.concrete: Two-layer academic diagram with human spatial writing on top and machine serialization below, clearly linked but distinct, clean white background
image_prompts.abstract: A spatial symbol field above and a linear code stream below, connected through translation

bullets: Human writing concerns composition and reading
Machine encoding concerns storage and computation
Plane writing can still require serialized software representation
The two layers should not be confused
slides.speech:
At this point, one distinction becomes essential. Human writing and software encoding are not the same thing. Human writing concerns what writers compose, what readers perceive, and which visible relations matter linguistically. Software encoding concerns storage, transmission, search, sorting, parsing, and computation.

A writing system can be spatial for humans while still requiring serialization for software. That is not a contradiction. It is the normal technical problem of making a writing system usable in software. So when terms like prefix, signbox, string, or encoding appear later, they should be understood as technical responses to a plane-based writing system, not as evidence that the writing was really linear all along.
slides.notes:
This slide protects the rest of the talk from confusion.

Key points:
- human writing asks what the reader sees and what relations matter
- machine encoding asks how software stores and processes that writing
- serialization is a technical requirement, not a claim about the true geometry of the writing

If you lose your place:
- say a map can be stored in data structures without becoming a sentence
- the same principle applies here

Transition:
- now show the geometry through concrete cases before returning to the technical solution.

slides.title: Comparative Cases
slides.summary: Different literacy pathways become clearer when point, line, and plane are compared through concrete cases.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for comparative writing cases.

image_prompts.concrete: Four-panel academic comparison showing hearing child with print, deaf child with SignWriting, specialist spatial notation like math or music, and video-only sign documentation, clear and restrained
image_prompts.abstract: Four pathways branching from one literacy field, with line and plane contrasted

bullets: Hearing child plus print literacy
Deaf child plus SignWriting literacy
Spatial specialist notation such as math or music
Video or transcription without everyday writable text
slides.speech:
The framework becomes clearer when we compare concrete literacy pathways. A hearing child typically develops spoken language first and then line-based print literacy. The written pathway is sequential text. A deaf child may develop signed language first and then potentially gain literacy through a writable plane-based system. There the written pathway is spatial text.

A third case reminds us that spatial literacy is not bizarre. Readers already use mathematics, music, chemistry, and other specialist notations in which placement, grouping, and vertical relation carry meaning. But the fourth case is the one that matters most for this talk. Video and researcher transcription are valuable forms of documentation. They are not the same as everyday readable and writable text. They preserve language for study or record. They do not by themselves provide the written pathway that a writing system provides.
slides.notes:
Take one sentence per case and keep the contrast crisp.

Case logic:
- hearing child plus print: durable line pathway
- deaf child plus SignWriting: durable plane pathway
- specialist notation: proof that spatial literacy is already real
- video/transcription: documentation, not everyday writable text

If pressed on the contrast case:
- say the issue is not value versus no value
- it is durable text versus documentation and analysis

Transition:
- now state the structural gap in one plain sentence.

slides.title: The Structural Gap
slides.summary: Signed languages are fully linguistic, but they have often lacked equally durable writable pathways.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for the structural gap in signed-language writing.

image_prompts.concrete: Diagram showing full signed language on one side, video and transcription tools in the middle, and a missing writable plane-based pathway on the other, clear and restrained
image_prompts.abstract: A strong language system separated from durable text infrastructure by a visible gap

bullets: Signed languages are full human languages
Video and transcription are useful but not sufficient
Writable plane-based text is the missing layer
The gap affects literacy, corpora, and infrastructure
slides.speech:
Here is the core problem. Signed languages are full human languages, but they have often lacked equally mature written pathways. Instead, the work has often depended on video, gloss, or researcher-facing transcription.

Those tools are useful and worth keeping. But they solve different problems. Video records performance. Transcription supports analysis. Neither automatically gives a community everyday readable and writable text. That missing layer has consequences for literacy, editing, comparison, corpus building, lexicons, annotation, computation, and long-term infrastructure. So the question is not whether signed languages are rich enough for writing. The question is whether they have been given durable pathways of the right shape.
slides.notes:
State the gap plainly and calmly.

Key points:
- signed languages are not missing linguistic structure
- they are missing equally mature writable pathways
- video and transcription remain useful, but they solve different problems

Useful fallback phrase:
- this is a structural disadvantage, not a linguistic deficiency

Transition:
- now move to the resolution: what SignWriting already proves in practice.

slides.title: What SignWriting Already Demonstrates
slides.summary: SignWriting provides plane-based text, and Formal SignWriting provides a way to preserve both spatial composition and sequence-sensitive information computationally.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for SignWriting and Formal SignWriting.

image_prompts.concrete: Technical yet accessible diagram showing a SignWriting signbox on one side and a structured Formal SignWriting representation on the other, modern standards-style graphic
image_prompts.abstract: A spatial sign crystallizing into a structured encoded form without losing its shape

bullets: Readable and writable plane-based text already exists
The signbox preserves meaningful spatial relations
Formal SignWriting preserves both sequence-sensitive and spatial information
Plane writing can therefore be stored and processed as text
slides.speech:
SignWriting already demonstrates that signed language can be written as plane-based text. A written sign is not merely a row of symbolic labels. It is a meaningful signbox whose spatial composition matters to the writing itself.

Formal SignWriting extends that achievement computationally. A useful plain-language description is that a sign can be encoded as a two-part word: a temporal prefix and a spatial signbox. The temporal prefix preserves sequence-sensitive information. The spatial signbox preserves the composed written form. That matters because it shows that plane writing can be stored and processed as text without collapsing the writing into a simple line. This is not just a storage trick. It is an encoding strategy adequate to the writing itself.
slides.notes:
This is the technical resolution slide. Treat it with confidence.

Explain it in plain language first:
- SignWriting gives visible plane-based text
- Formal SignWriting gives a machine-readable way to preserve that text
- one part handles sequence-sensitive information
- one part handles the spatial composition of the sign

If you need a very short fallback:
- the writing stays planar for humans and becomes tractable for software

Transition:
- now widen from proof of concept to ecosystem consequences.

slides.title: What Becomes Possible
slides.summary: Once plane-based writing can be stored and processed as text, it can support literacy materials, corpora, tools, datasets, and new research programs.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for what becomes possible with encoded plane writing.

image_prompts.concrete: Research ecosystem diagram linking plane writing to bilingual materials, corpora, lexicons, annotation, datasets, AI, and experiments, polished academic style
image_prompts.abstract: A spatial writing field turning into tools, corpora, experiments, and infrastructure across a network

bullets: Search, sorting, and corpora become practical
Lexicons, annotation, and datasets become more structured
Bilingual literacy materials become easier to build
New computational and cognitive studies become possible
slides.speech:
Once plane-based writing can be stored and processed as text, the ecosystem changes. Search becomes practical. Sorting becomes practical. Corpora, lexicons, annotation pipelines, and structured datasets become easier to build, share, and reuse.

That matters far beyond software. It matters for literacy materials, bilingual educational work, linguistics, human-computer interaction, AI, and cognitive research. The point is not only that SignWriting can be displayed on a screen. The point is that it can participate in a larger research and technical support system that respects the writing itself.
slides.notes:
Let this slide widen the stakes.

Key points:
- move from writing theory to usable infrastructure
- name both literacy outcomes and research outcomes
- emphasize reuse, not just display

If you need examples:
- searchable corpora
- structured lexicons
- bilingual materials
- experimental stimuli for research

Transition:
- after the upside, show intellectual discipline by separating strong claims from open questions.

slides.title: Strong Claims and Open Questions
slides.summary: The shape-of-writing claim is strong; the cognitive, educational, and NLP consequences remain open research questions.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for strong claims and open questions.

image_prompts.concrete: Academic split diagram with strong conceptual claims at top and open research questions below, restrained and professional
image_prompts.abstract: A firm geometric framework beside a field of open research questions, scholarly and balanced

bullets: How writing uses space is a strong conceptual claim
SignWriting as plane writing is a strong interpretive claim
Reading, literacy, and NLP consequences remain open
Keeping levels separate strengthens the work
slides.speech:
I want to keep the levels of claim clear. The strong claim is that writing systems differ in how they use space, that point, line, and plane are useful analytic tendencies, that SignWriting is a genuine case of plane-based writing, and that Formal SignWriting provides a way to preserve that writing in software. That is already a substantial claim.

The open questions are different. How do readers process plane-based writing compared with line-based writing? Which spatial relations are most cognitively salient? How should plane writing be tokenized for NLP and machine learning? What new experimental stimuli become possible once spatial composition can be stored and processed as text? Those are not weaknesses in the work. They are the research frontier created by taking the writing seriously. Keeping the strong claims and the open questions separate is what makes the argument rigorous rather than promotional.
slides.notes:
This slide gives the whole deck credibility.

Strong claims to name clearly:
- writing is not inherently linear
- point, line, and plane are useful analytic tendencies
- SignWriting is plane-based writing
- Formal SignWriting preserves the plane instead of flattening it away

Open questions to name briefly:
- reading and processing
- literacy development
- NLP tokenization and representation
- interface and experiment design

Transition:
- close by tying the shape of writing back to the modality argument from Language Without Sound.

slides.title: Conclusion / Contact / Collaboration
slides.summary: Signed-language plane writing belongs in the broader science of writing as a core case study rather than an exception.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for conclusion and collaboration.

image_prompts.concrete: Closing academic keynote image uniting point line and plane writing geometries with SignWriting highlighted as a modern spatial system, elegant and confident
image_prompts.abstract: Point line and plane converging into one luminous theory of writing with spatial sign symbols in the foreground

bullets: Language is not inherently auditory
Writing is not inherently linear
Signed languages need durable plane-based pathways
The next step is research, tools, datasets, and infrastructure
slides.speech:
The series opened by arguing that language is not inherently auditory. This second step argues that writing is not inherently linear. Point systems show integration. Line systems show sequence. Plane systems show spatial composition. Together, those two claims clear space for a much more accurate understanding of signed-language literacy.

Once that is clear, the question changes. The issue is no longer whether SignWriting looks unusual from the perspective of alphabetic print. The issue is how to study, support, encode, and build with a genuine spatial writing system. That means research on reading and cognition. It means better tools, datasets, corpora, and literacy materials. It means treating plane-based writing as something that deserves real support rather than dismissal. That is the collaboration I am looking for: science, tools, and research support equal to the shape of the writing itself.
slides.notes:
Close as an invitation, not a defense.

Key structure:
- first talk: language does not require sound
- second talk: writing does not require linearity
- together they justify durable signed-language literacy pathways and infrastructure

If you want the shortest closing line:
- the task now is not to justify the possibility of plane writing, but to build with it responsibly

Delivery note:
- pause after the first paragraph, then let the final sentence land cleanly.
